questionText: Adapter vs Decorator: which statement is TRUE?
options: Adapter changes an object's interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and adds behavior
Decorator changes the interface; Adapter keeps it the same
correctAnswers: Adapter changes an object's interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and adds behavior
explanation: Adapter converts an interface to a different expected one; Decorator preserves the interface and augments behavior.

questionText: Complete the Flyweight factory GlyphFactory that caches shared Glyph objects.
explanation: The factory reuses cached flyweights, only creating and storing a new shared object when none exists for the key.

questionText: Match each code-coverage metric to its definition.
matchingOptions: Percentage of functions or methods that have been called at least once
Percentage of executable statements that have been run at least once
Percentage of individual boolean sub-conditions evaluated both true and false
Percentage of independent end-to-end execution paths through the code that have been taken

correctAnswers.Condition coverage: Percentage of individual boolean sub-conditions evaluated both true and false
correctAnswers.Function coverage: Percentage of functions or methods that have been called at least once
correctAnswers.Path coverage: Percentage of independent end-to-end execution paths through the code that have been taken
correctAnswers.Statement coverage: Percentage of executable statements that have been run at least once

explanation: Standard coverage metric definitions.

questionText: Proxy vs Decorator: which statement is TRUE?
options: Proxy controls access to an object; Decorator adds responsibilities to it
Decorator controls access; Proxy adds responsibilities
correctAnswers: Proxy controls access to an object; Decorator adds responsibilities to it
explanation: Same wrapping structure, different intent: Proxy is about access control/laziness, Decorator about adding behavior.

questionText: Complete the Flyweight factory ParticleFactory that caches shared Particle objects.
explanation: The factory reuses cached flyweights, only creating and storing a new shared object when none exists for the key.

questionText: Match each code-coverage metric to its definition.
matchingOptions: Percentage of functions or methods that have been called at least once
Percentage of decision outcomes (true/false of each branch) that have been exercised
Percentage of individual boolean sub-conditions evaluated both true and false
Percentage of independent end-to-end execution paths through the code that have been taken

correctAnswers.Branch coverage: Percentage of decision outcomes (true/false of each branch) that have been exercised
correctAnswers.Condition coverage: Percentage of individual boolean sub-conditions evaluated both true and false
correctAnswers.Function coverage: Percentage of functions or methods that have been called at least once
correctAnswers.Path coverage: Percentage of independent end-to-end execution paths through the code that have been taken

explanation: Standard coverage metric definitions.

questionText: Which problems can affect the Simple Factory shown? Select 1 or more answers
options: Adding a new product type requires modifying the factory (OCP)
An unknown type string can silently return null
Passing raw strings is not type-safe
It guarantees a single instance of each product
correctAnswers: Adding a new product type requires modifying the factory (OCP)
An unknown type string can silently return null
Passing raw strings is not type-safe
The if/switch chain grows unwieldy
explanation: Simple Factory centralises creation but couples new types to edits, risks null, is string-unsafe, and grows messy. It says nothing about single instances.

explanation: The factory reuses cached flyweights, only creating and storing a new shared object when none exists for the key.

questionText: Why is the template method commonly declared final in the Template Method pattern?
options: To stop subclasses from changing the algorithm's step order
To allow subclasses to override the whole algorithm
To make the class a singleton
correctAnswers: To stop subclasses from changing the algorithm's step order
explanation: final locks the skeleton; subclasses may only fill in the individual abstract steps.

questionText: Complete the Bridge: the Shape abstraction delegates drawing to the Renderer implementation.
explanation: Bridge holds a reference to a separate implementor hierarchy and forwards the work to it, so both sides vary independently.

questionText: What is the main problem with this lazy Singleton in a multithreaded program?
options: It is not thread-safe: two threads can both create an instance
It violates the Liskov Substitution Principle
It breaks encapsulation by exposing the field
It cannot have a private constructor
correctAnswers: It is not thread-safe: two threads can both create an instance
explanation: The check-then-create is a race condition; without synchronization two threads can each create an instance.

questionText: Complete the Bridge: the Remote abstraction delegates drawing to the Device implementation.
explanation: Bridge holds a reference to a separate implementor hierarchy and forwards the work to it, so both sides vary independently.

questionText: Which technique makes the lazy Singleton getInstance() thread-safe while avoiding locking on every call? Select 1 or more answers
options: Double-checked locking with a volatile instance field
Eager initialization of the static instance
correctAnswers: Double-checked locking with a volatile instance field
Eager initialization of the static instance
Using an enum singleton
explanation: Volatile double-checked locking, eager init, and enum singletons are all valid thread-safe approaches; removing the check or a public constructor break the pattern.

questionText: Complete the Bridge: the Shape2 abstraction delegates drawing to the Color implementation.
explanation: Bridge holds a reference to a separate implementor hierarchy and forwards the work to it, so both sides vary independently.

questionText: Match each kind of test double to its definition.
matchingOptions: A working but lightweight implementation, such as an in-memory database
A real object wrapper that also records information about how it was called
A pre-programmed object with expectations that verifies the calls it receives
An object passed around but never actually used, only to fill a parameter list
Provides canned, predefined answers to calls made during the test

correctAnswers.Dummy: An object passed around but never actually used, only to fill a parameter list
correctAnswers.Stub: Provides canned, predefined answers to calls made during the test
correctAnswers.Mock: A pre-programmed object with expectations that verifies the calls it receives
correctAnswers.Spy: A real object wrapper that also records information about how it was called
correctAnswers.Fake: A working but lightweight implementation, such as an in-memory database

explanation: The five Meszaros test-double categories.

questionText: In a Flyweight, where should the extrinsic (context-specific) state be kept?
options: Outside the shared object, supplied by the client per call
Inside the shared object as immutable fields
It should be duplicated into every flyweight
correctAnswers: Outside the shared object, supplied by the client per call
explanation: Flyweight stores only shared intrinsic state internally and pushes extrinsic state out to the caller.

questionText: Complete the Mediator pattern: User reports events to the ChatMediator.
explanation: Colleagues send their events to the central mediator instead of talking to each other directly.

questionText: Which GoF category is NOT one of the three defined in the Gang of Four book?
correctAnswers: Architectural
explanation: GoF defines Creational, Structural and Behavioral; Architectural is a separate, broader concept.

questionText: Complete the Mediator pattern: Aircraft reports events to the Tower.
explanation: Colleagues send their events to the central mediator instead of talking to each other directly.

questionText: Which single principle states that high-level modules and low-level modules should both depend on abstractions?
correctAnswers: Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)
explanation: That is the definition of the Dependency Inversion Principle.

questionText: Complete the Mediator pattern: Widget reports events to the DialogMediator.
explanation: Colleagues send their events to the central mediator instead of talking to each other directly.

questionText: Path coverage is generally stronger (harder to fully achieve) than which of the following? Select 1 or more answers
correctAnswers: Statement coverage
Branch coverage
explanation: Path coverage subsumes branch coverage, which subsumes statement coverage; the others are unrelated.

questionText: Complete the Visitor double-dispatch in Circle.accept().
explanation: accept() takes the visitor and calls back visit(this); the argument type selects the right overload (double dispatch).

questionText: You must build a small rule engine that parses and evaluates boolean expressions such as '(active AND premium) OR trial' written by business users in a custom mini-language. Which single design pattern is most associated with this?
correctAnswers: Interpreter
explanation: Defining a grammar for a little language and an evaluator for its sentences is the Interpreter pattern.

questionText: Complete the Visitor double-dispatch in FileNode.accept().
explanation: accept() takes the visitor and calls back visit(this); the argument type selects the right overload (double dispatch).

questionText: A search box lets users type a tiny query language ('price<100 AND brand=acme') that your code must parse into an expression tree and evaluate against each product. Which single design pattern fits best?
correctAnswers: Interpreter
explanation: Representing grammar rules as classes and evaluating the resulting expression tree is the Interpreter pattern.

questionText: Complete the Interpreter pattern where AddExpression adds its two sub-expressions.
explanation: Each terminal/non-terminal implements the common Expression interface; a binary expression interprets its children and combines them with its operator.

questionText: Match each testing technique to its description.
matchingOptions: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur
Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior
Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
Divides inputs into classes that should be treated the same and tests one value per class

correctAnswers.Boundary value analysis: Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
correctAnswers.Equivalence partitioning: Divides inputs into classes that should be treated the same and tests one value per class
correctAnswers.Cyclomatic complexity: Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
correctAnswers.Cardinality testing: Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
correctAnswers.Regression testing: Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior
correctAnswers.Error-condition testing: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur

explanation: Core black-box / white-box testing techniques.

questionText: Which design pattern does the following code implement?
correctAnswers: Interpreter
explanation: An Expression interface with interpret(), terminal Number expressions and non-terminal operator expressions forming an AST is the Interpreter pattern.

questionText: Which statement best describes the Interpreter pattern?
options: It defines a representation for a grammar plus an interpreter that evaluates sentences in that language
It provides sequential access to a collection's elements
It adds responsibilities to an object by wrapping it
It ensures a class has only one instance
correctAnswers: It defines a representation for a grammar plus an interpreter that evaluates sentences in that language
explanation: Interpreter models grammar rules as a class hierarchy and evaluates expression trees built from them.

questionText: Complete the Interpreter pattern where MultiplyExpression multiplies its two sub-expressions.
explanation: Each terminal/non-terminal implements the common Expression interface; a binary expression interprets its children and combines them with its operator.

correctAnswers.Cardinality testing: Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
correctAnswers.Cyclomatic complexity: Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
correctAnswers.Equivalence partitioning: Divides inputs into classes that should be treated the same and tests one value per class
correctAnswers.Error-condition testing: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur

explanation: Core black-box / white-box testing techniques.

questionText: In a typical Interpreter implementation, which roles usually appear? Select 1 or more answers
options: An abstract Expression with an interpret() method
Non-terminal expressions (e.g., add, multiply) holding sub-expressions
A single static instance shared globally
A cloning method to copy the grammar
correctAnswers: An abstract Expression with an interpret() method
Terminal expressions (e.g., numbers, variables)
Non-terminal expressions (e.g., add, multiply) holding sub-expressions
explanation: Interpreter has an abstract expression plus terminal and non-terminal expression classes; the other options describe Singleton/Prototype.

questionText: Complete the Interpreter pattern where DivideExpression divides its two sub-expressions.
explanation: Each terminal/non-terminal implements the common Expression interface; a binary expression interprets its children and combines them with its operator.

Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)

correctAnswers.Regression testing: Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior

questionText: A social network must let callers walk a user's friends list one by one (and later a paginated server-backed version) without ever exposing whether the data is an array, a linked list, or a remote cursor. Which single design pattern fits best?
correctAnswers: Iterator
explanation: Providing uniform sequential access while hiding the underlying storage is the Iterator pattern.
